hypothesis: The bucketed locality bias that improved the K=16 point may also strengthen the low-to-mid-K lane at K=32 under EF=16 by reducing cross-bucket competition without the instability of full routing.
summary: Round 125 produced a real frontier improvement at K=16 with `bucketed_lowrank_two_stage_soft_codebook_residual`, so the next disciplined step is to map whether that gain extends to the adjacent frontier-relevant K=32 slot. This keeps the architecture and recipe fixed while changing only K, which is the highest-value follow-up for determining whether the new family deserves promotion beyond a single-point win.
self_review: This is the correct post-win follow-up: hold architecture and recipe constant, move to the nearest valuable K slot, and test whether the improvement generalizes into the low-to-mid-K frontier. The main risk is that the bucketed inductive bias helps only at very small K, but that is exactly what this round will resolve.
notes_to_memory: Round 126 follows the new K=16 improvement by mapping the same bucketed family at K=32 under the fixed EF=16 regime.
Hold architecture and recipe fixed so attribution stays on the K-axis after the K=16 win.
A competitive K=32 result would indicate the bucketed family is not just a one-off small-K repair but a broader frontier candidate in the EF=16 regime.
next_hypotheses: If the bucketed family is competitive at K=32, test K=24 or K=64 next to map the extent of its frontier coverage under EF=16.
If K=32 is clearly worse while K=16 remains strong, treat the bucketed family as a specialized small-K architecture and probe K=24 rather than pushing higher.
If K=32 nearly matches the incumbent but is noisy, run one confirmatory repeat at K=32 before promoting the family further.
